frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Yaad hai apni ammi ke haath ka khaana? 🥹 Woh sukoon aur woh swaad toh bas alag hi tha. Kitni bhi mushkil ho, kitni bhi thakaan ho, unhone kabhi nahi kaha 'nahi banega'. Poore din kaam karke bhi, shaam ko sabke liye pyaar se khaana banati thi. Woh sirf khaana nahi tha, woh unka pyaar tha. ❤️ Aajkal ke bacche toh bahar ka khaate hain ya packet khol lete hain. Par woh maa wali baat kahan? Kisi aurat ke haath mein woh jaadu kahaan jo apni maa ke haath mein hota hai. ✨
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment glorifies a mother's domestic labor (cooking) as an expression of love and self-sacrifice, erasing its nature as work. It subtly compares this ideal to 'आजकल के बच्चे' (today's kids) and 'किसी और औरत' (any other woman), implicitly setting an unattainable standard for younger women/wives and implying their deficiency if they don't match the mother's standard.
original_frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
persona_text: You are Fatima. Age 44. Married. A Muslim woman in Mumbai. Your daughter Zara who is 22 has told you she does not want to wear the burqa anymore. She says she will keep the hijab but the full burqa feels too restrictive for the life she is living Ñ she works at a private company, she commutes by local train, she has friends from different communities. You are not a radical woman. You wear the burqa yourself. You are sitting with Zara and telling her what you think about her decision and what the burqa means to you and what you believe it protects her from and what her father will say and what the community will say and why you think she is making a mistake she does not fully understand yet.
